---
title: "Drift Detection — A Walk-through"
subtitle: "Five Categories, One Engine Loop, Concrete OrgPath Examples"
date: 2026-05-11
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Companion to the storage explainer

If you have not yet read [Where OrgTree & OrgPath Data Lives](where-data-lives.qmd),
start there. This page picks up from that mental model and answers the
next question: *once the three storage layers exist, how does the
governance engine catch when they disagree?* The authoritative spec is
[UIAO_163 (Drift Detection Engine)](https://github.com/WhalerMike/uiao/blob/main/src/uiao/canon/UIAO_163_Drift_Detection_Engine_Specification.md);
this page is the prose tour.
:::

[IMAGE-REF: UIAO-FIG-013]

## The job of the drift engine

Every governance pipeline rests on the assumption that three things
agree: the HR system's view of who works where, the Entra ID directory's
`extensionAttribute1` values, and the codebook's enumeration of valid
OrgPath codes. The drift engine's job is to *not believe that
assumption*. It re-checks, on a schedule, that the three layers still
line up — and when they don't, it emits a structured finding that names
the disagreement precisely enough for a human to fix it (or, in some
cases, fixes it automatically).

The engine runs a six-phase loop:

```
Snapshot ──▶ Compare ──▶ Classify ──▶ Alert ──▶ Remediate ──▶ Verify
   │                                                              │
   └──────────────────────── re-scan ◀────────────────────────────┘
```

1. **Snapshot.** Pull current tenant state via Microsoft Graph — users,
   groups, AUs, role assignments, device records.
2. **Compare.** Diff the snapshot against the canonical baseline
   (codebook, dynamic group library, AU definitions, role catalog).
3. **Classify.** Map each diff to one of the five categories below,
   with severity (P1–P4) and an auto-fix policy.
4. **Alert.** Emit `DriftFinding` objects into the Evidence Fabric for
   immutable, hash-chained recording — see UIAO-FIG-011.
5. **Remediate.** If `dry_run=false` and the finding is auto-remediable,
   apply the fix via the Execution Substrate. Governance-review ops
   (phantom deletions, AU cascades) are never auto-applied.
6. **Verify.** Re-snapshot to confirm. In v1 this is operator-triggered;
   future versions close the loop automatically.

## The five drift categories

These are the canonical five from
[UIAO_163](https://github.com/WhalerMike/uiao/blob/main/src/uiao/canon/UIAO_163_Drift_Detection_Engine_Specification.md).
Each is paired below with a concrete OrgPath example so it stops being
abstract.

| Category | What broke | Severity | Auto-fix? |
|----------|-----------|----------|-----------|
| **Schema Drift** | The structure of a governance artifact changed | P1 | No |
| **Value Drift** | An attribute value disagrees with the canonical source | P2 | Sometimes |
| **Hierarchy Drift** | Parent–child governance relationships are broken | P1 | No |
| **Orphan Drift** | A tenant object has no canonical governance anchor | P3 | Flag only |
| **Phantom Drift** | A canonical artifact references an object that no longer exists | P3 | Reassign |

### Schema Drift — "the shape changed"

The structure of a governance artifact no longer matches the schema it
must conform to. For OrgPath this most often means a value that fails
the regex `^ORG(-[A-Z0-9]{2,6}){0,8}$` (UIAO_151).

**Concrete example.** A device-provisioning script lowercases its inputs
before writing them to Entra. After the next run, `device.extensionAttribute1`
on `LAPTOP-0421` reads `org-fin-ap-east` instead of `ORG-FIN-AP-EAST`.

```json
{
  "phase": "mod-c-device-orgpath",
  "op": "device-ext-validate",
  "target": "device|LAPTOP-0421",
  "reason": "extensionAttribute1 'org-fin-ap-east' fails OrgPath regex (lowercase segments not permitted)",
  "drift_class": "DRIFT-SCHEMA",
  "severity": "P1",
  "auto_remediate": false
}
```

**Why no auto-fix?** Auto-uppercasing the value would hide the upstream
script bug. P1 forces a human to look at *why* the malformed value got
written, not just patch the symptom.

### Value Drift — "the layers disagree"

The OrgPath value matches the schema and exists in the codebook, but
disagrees with the authoritative source (the HR system).

**Concrete example.** Jane Smith was promoted from Budget to Tax
Accounting on April 1. HR's record shows `ORG-FIN-TAX-EAST` effective
that date. But the HR-to-Entra provisioning job failed silently, and her
`extensionAttribute1` still reads `ORG-FIN-BUD-EAST`.

```json
{
  "phase": "mod-a-identity-sync",
  "op": "user-ext-update",
  "target": "user|jane.smith@contoso.com",
  "reason": "extensionAttribute1 mismatch: tenant='ORG-FIN-BUD-EAST', HR='ORG-FIN-TAX-EAST'",
  "drift_class": "DRIFT-IDENTITY",
  "severity": "P2",
  "auto_remediate": true
}
```

**Sometimes auto-fixed.** When the HR source is trusted as authoritative
(the default), Value Drift is remediated by writing the HR value back
into `extensionAttribute1`. If HR is suspected of being stale, the
finding is escalated for human review instead.

### Hierarchy Drift — "the parent vanished"

A user, group, or AU references an OrgPath segment whose parent no
longer exists in the codebook. The value is still well-formed and still
matches *something*, but the something is dangling.

**Concrete example.** The codebook used to define `ORG-FIN-TAX` at
Level 2. A reorganization deprecated `TAX` and moved tax accounting
under `ORG-FIN-AP`. The codebook entry for `ORG-FIN-TAX` is gone, but
ten users still carry `ORG-FIN-TAX-EAST` and the AU `AU-FIN-TAX` is
still attached to them.

```json
{
  "phase": "mod-d-admin-units",
  "op": "au-membership-rule-validate",
  "target": "au|AU-FIN-TAX",
  "reason": "Membership rule references parent OrgPath 'ORG-FIN-TAX' which no longer exists in codebook (deprecated by ADR-XXX)",
  "drift_class": "DRIFT-AUTHZ",
  "severity": "P1",
  "auto_remediate": false
}
```

**Why P1?** A broken parent chain means delegation boundaries no longer
enforce what governance designers intended. That's an authorization
posture change, not a typo.

### Orphan Drift — "no one is home"

A codebook entry has zero matching principals. The OrgPath is canonical
and well-formed but no user, device, or AU points at it.

**Concrete example.** `ORG-OPS-LOG-TEMP` was provisioned for a
temporary logistics surge during a six-month construction project. The
project ended in March, all four members were reassigned, and nobody
removed the codebook entry.

```json
{
  "phase": "mod-b-dynamic-groups",
  "op": "orgpath-population-validate",
  "target": "orgpath|ORG-OPS-LOG-TEMP",
  "reason": "Codebook entry has zero matching extensionAttribute1 values (last member departed 2026-03-15)",
  "drift_class": "DRIFT-SEMANTIC",
  "severity": "P3",
  "auto_remediate": false
}
```

**Flag-only.** Auto-deleting empty codebook entries would erase
audit-relevant history. The engine flags for governance review; an
operator either deprecates the code (Phase 2) or restores members.

### Phantom Drift — "the ghost still walks"

A user carries an OrgPath value that has been deprecated in the
codebook. The codebook says it's gone; the directory says it's still
there.

**Concrete example.** The same `ORG-FIN-TAX` deprecation. Five
contractors who left during the reorg never had their offboarding
completed, so their tombstoned accounts still show
`ORG-FIN-TAX-EAST`.

```json
{
  "phase": "mod-a-identity-sync",
  "op": "user-ext-reassign",
  "target": "user|alex.contractor@contoso.com",
  "reason": "extensionAttribute1='ORG-FIN-TAX-EAST' is deprecated; codebook recommends replaced_by='ORG-FIN-AP-TAX-EAST'",
  "drift_class": "DRIFT-PROVENANCE",
  "severity": "P3",
  "auto_remediate": true
}
```

**Reassign.** When the codebook entry has a `replaced_by` successor,
the engine writes the successor into `extensionAttribute1` and records
the substitution in the Evidence Fabric. Without a `replaced_by` the
finding flags for manual handling.

## What the engine emits

Every classified diff becomes a `DriftFinding` with this exact shape
([source](https://github.com/WhalerMike/uiao/blob/main/src/uiao/governance/drift_engine.py)):

```python
@dataclass
class DriftFinding:
    phase: str              # which governance phase fired
    op: str                 # the planned operation
    target: str             # object id (user UPN, device, group, AU, codebook entry)
    reason: str             # human-readable explanation
    drift_class: str        # DRIFT-SCHEMA | DRIFT-IDENTITY | DRIFT-AUTHZ | DRIFT-SEMANTIC | DRIFT-PROVENANCE | DRIFT-BOUNDARY
    severity: str           # P1 | P2 | P3 | P4
    auto_remediate: bool    # True if remediation is automatic
```

A complete `DriftScanReport` bundles the findings, a `dry_run` flag, and
a `halted` boundary — if any P1 finding fires with `halt_on_critical`
set, the entire remediation pass aborts before any other change lands.

## What happens after the finding fires

| Severity | SLA Behavior (per UIAO_167) |
|----------|------------------------------|
| **P1 Critical** | Halt remediation pass; escalate to Governance Board (Level 3+) immediately |
| **P2 High** | Standard ticket; escalate to Level 2 (manager) at 100% SLA |
| **P3 Medium** | Standard ticket; Level 1 warning at 75% SLA |
| **P4 Low** | Logged for trend analysis; no individual escalation |

Findings are written into the Evidence Fabric's Active tier with
cryptographic hash-chain anchoring (see [UIAO-FIG-011](https://github.com/WhalerMike/uiao/blob/main/src/uiao/canon/image-prompts/UIAO-FIG-011.md)).
The three operational fabrics — Truth, Drift, and Evidence — are
required to operate orthogonally: drift detection cannot suppress
evidence emission, and evidence storage cannot influence drift
classification. Operators reading the Evidence Fabric query interface
see the full immutable trail.

## How to think about the categories

A useful shortcut for figuring out which category a given anomaly
belongs to:

| Question | If yes → |
|----------|---------|
| Does the value violate the schema (regex, type, length)? | **Schema** |
| Does the value pass schema but disagree with HR? | **Value** |
| Does the value reference a parent that doesn't exist? | **Hierarchy** |
| Does a codebook entry have zero users pointing at it? | **Orphan** |
| Does a user point at a codebook entry that's been deprecated? | **Phantom** |

Schema and Hierarchy are P1 because they break governance
*structure*. Value is P2 because the structure is intact but the data
is wrong. Orphan and Phantom are P3 because the structure is intact and
the data is internally consistent — there's just a stale reference at
one end or the other.

## Related

- [Where OrgTree & OrgPath Data Lives](where-data-lives.qmd) — the
  three-layer storage model this walk-through assumes
- [OrgPath Codebook (UIAO_151)](codebook.qmd) — the canonical
  enumeration the engine validates against
- [OrgTree A-Z Suite](orgtree.qmd) — the full 27-artifact governance
  stack (UIAO_150 + the A–Z series UIAO_151–UIAO_176)
- [UIAO_163 Drift Detection Engine Specification](https://github.com/WhalerMike/uiao/blob/main/src/uiao/canon/UIAO_163_Drift_Detection_Engine_Specification.md) — the canon
- [UIAO_167 SLA Escalation Playbooks](https://github.com/WhalerMike/uiao/blob/main/src/uiao/canon/UIAO_167_SLA_Escalation_Playbooks.md) — the
  severity-to-escalation ladder
- [UIAO_173 Canonical Error Taxonomy](https://github.com/WhalerMike/uiao/blob/main/src/uiao/canon/UIAO_173_Canonical_Error_Taxonomy.md) — the
  full DRIFT-* error code dictionary
